title: Family prepares ecclesiastical career
summary: Pico was groomed from childhood for a church career, a background later scholarship uses to complicate stories of sudden religious conversion.
source_note: Walden notes Pico was groomed for an ecclesiastical career from childhood.

title: Born into a princely household
summary: Pico's noble status shaped his education, ecclesiastical prospects, patronage access, and the unusual freedom with which he moved among universities and courts.
source_note: Howlett, Life and Works; Copenhaver, modern memory chapters.

title: Birth at Mirandola
summary: Giovanni Pico is born into the ruling Pico family of Mirandola, a small but politically connected lordship in northern Italy.
source_note: Basic chronology; verify against biographical chapter and DBI.

title: Humanist education begins
summary: Pico receives the kind of elite grammatical, rhetorical, and classical education expected of a northern Italian noble prodigy.
source_note: Needs full source anchoring in life chapter.

title: Studies canon law at Bologna
summary: As a teenager Pico is associated with Bologna, where legal study would have suited the ecclesiastical career planned for him.
source_note: Walden places Pico and Savonarola at Bologna in the late 1470s.

title: Overlap with Savonarola's educational world
summary: Pico and Savonarola probably first occupy the same intellectual geography while studying in Bologna.

title: Ferrara connection
summary: Pico's path intersects with Ferrara, an Este court/university context also important to Savonarola's formation.

title: Possible encounter with Savonarola at Reggio Emilia
summary: A definite encounter is reported around 1480 when the young Pico was invited to a Dominican chapter for a disputation by Savonarola.

title: Pico hears Dominican disputation culture
summary: The Reggio Emilia episode places Pico in a scholastic-religious disputation setting before the Roman controversy of 1486-87.
source_note: Inference from Walden's Reggio Emilia note.

title: Studies in Padua
summary: Pico's philosophical formation is associated with Padua, a key Aristotelian university environment.
source_note: Needs precise source anchor from Howlett/Copenhaver.

title: Aristotelian formation intensifies
summary: Pico's later concordist work depends on serious Aristotelian and scholastic training, not only Florentine Platonism.
source_note: Howlett frames the need to re-evaluate Pico through Aristotelianism, Kabbalism, and Platonism.

title: Northern Italian university circuit
summary: Pico's movement among Bologna, Ferrara, Padua, and perhaps Pavia places him in a mobile university culture before his Florentine fame.

title: Studies at Pavia
summary: Howlett places Pico at Pavia University from autumn 1482 into 1483; Pavia is important for logic, mathematics, and scholastic philosophy.
source_note: Howlett, Life and Works, extracted p. 24.

title: Contacts with Florence deepen
summary: Pico comes increasingly into the orbit of Florentine humanists, Medici patronage, and Ficinian Platonism.

title: Turns toward Hebrew and Kabbalistic sources
summary: Pico's mature project begins to draw on Jewish sources, Hebrew learning, and Kabbalistic materials that later become central to Pico studies.
source_note: Howlett preface emphasizes later Pico studies' turn toward Kabbalism.

title: Contacts with Jewish intellectuals
summary: Pico's Kabbalistic and Hebrew interests require mediators, translators, and interlocutors such as Flavius Mithridates and wider Jewish learned networks.
source_note: Copenhaver Magic and Dignity contents: Secrets and Codes, Counting on Flavius.

title: Composes and gathers theses
summary: Pico begins assembling the enormous concordist program that will become the 900 Conclusions.

title: Concord becomes a public project
summary: The project to reconcile philosophical and theological traditions moves from private study toward public disputation.

title: Goes to Paris
summary: Wirszubski notes that Pico went to Paris in summer 1485 and returned to Italy in March 1486.

title: Writes the Oration
summary: Pico writes the text later famous as the Oration on the Dignity of Man, originally tied to the proposed Roman disputation.
source_note: Walden says in 1486 Pico wrote his Oration.

title: Publishes or circulates the 900 Conclusions
summary: Pico issues the 900 Conclusions as the basis for a spectacular public philosophical-theological disputation.

title: Travels toward Rome for disputation
summary: The Roman disputation project brings Pico's learned ambition into direct contact with papal authority.
source_note: General chronology; verify in Copenhaver On Trial.

title: Returns from Paris to Italy
summary: Pico returns to Italy from Paris, entering the decisive year of Arezzo, Perugia/La Fratta, Hebrew/Kabbalah study, and the 900 Conclusions.

title: Arezzo abduction scandal
summary: Howlett records Pico's attempt to abduct Margherita de' Medici at Arezzo, after which he was caught, she was rescued, and Pico was disgraced.
source_note: Howlett, Life and Works, extracted p. 27.

title: Elijah del Medigo joins Pico
summary: Howlett notes that Pico's old teacher Elijah del Medigo came to him while he was preparing the debate project.
source_note: Howlett, Life and Works, extracted p. 27.

title: Mithridates translations flow in
summary: Wirszubski identifies summer 1486 as the period when Flavius Mithridates' Kabbalistic translations for Pico flowed in great profusion.

title: Pico at Perugia
summary: Wirszubski uses a dated letter to Andrea Corneo to place Pico at Perugia on 15 October 1486.

title: Roman disputation planned
summary: Pico's proposed debate over the 900 Conclusions is expected to gather learned opponents in Rome.

title: 900 Theses printed in Rome
summary: Farmer states that the text of the 900 Theses was printed by Eucharius Silber in Rome on 7 December 1486.

title: Disputation rejected
summary: The planned debate over the 900 Theses is stopped, initiating the crisis that makes Pico a case for heresy, freedom, and philosophy.

title: Papal commission examines the theses
summary: A commission identifies problematic propositions among Pico's Conclusions, focusing attention on a subset later central to Copenhaver's trial analysis.
source_note: Copenhaver On Trial contents: Thirteen Conclusions.

title: Thirteen conclusions become focal
summary: The controversy concentrates on thirteen condemned or suspect conclusions, turning Pico's universal concord into a juridical-theological problem.

title: Pico writes the Apologia
summary: Pico produces a defense of his conclusions, sharpening the conflict with papal authority.
source_note: Apologia file present; Copenhaver On Trial bibliography abbreviates Apo. Pico (1487).

title: Flees toward France
summary: After the Roman project collapses, Pico flees toward France.

title: Imprisoned in France
summary: Pico is imprisoned in France as a heretic by papal servants after fleeing the failed Roman disputation.
source_note: Walden, p. 2; exact place needs verification.

title: French episode becomes part of the Pico myth
summary: The flight and imprisonment help turn Pico from prodigy into a dramatic figure of intellectual danger and ecclesiastical risk.

title: Lorenzo de' Medici secures safe harbor
summary: Lorenzo offers Pico safe harbor in Florence after the crisis.

title: Pico returns to Florence
summary: Pico's post-crisis life becomes increasingly tied to Florence, Medici protection, and later Savonarolan religion.

title: Religious intensification after papal danger
summary: Pico's renewed religious seriousness is linked by some scholarship to his brush with church authority rather than simple Savonarolan discipleship.

title: Vincennes as royal-protective detention
summary: Howlett reports that Pico was imprisoned at the royal palace of Vincennes, away from papal nuncios; this suggests detention under French royal protection as well as confinement.
source_note: Howlett, Life and Works, extracted p. 30.

title: Heptaplus
summary: Pico writes the Heptaplus, a sevenfold exposition of Genesis central to later debates over theology, Kabbalah, and posthumous editing.
source_note: Copenhaver On Trial abbreviates Hep. Pico (c.1489); Walden p. 1 describes Heptaplus.

title: Genesis becomes a major interpretive field
summary: Pico's Heptaplus shifts attention from the famous Oration to biblical hermeneutics and creation theology.

title: Savonarola returns to Florence
summary: Pico helps persuade Lorenzo to request Savonarola's presence at Florence.

title: Pico begins attending Savonarola's sermons
summary: Pico begins attending Savonarola's sermons and remains close to him until death.

title: Florence becomes Pico's late religious-intellectual setting
summary: Pico's late life is shaped by a triangular field of Medici patronage, Ficinian Platonism, and Savonarolan reform.

title: Disputes at San Marco
summary: Piero Crinito reports Pico and Savonarola engaging in regular intellectual disputes at San Marco's Greek library.

title: San Marco becomes a thinking site
summary: San Marco functions as more than a devotional space: it is a library, disputation setting, and later burial/memory site.
source_note: Inferred from Walden's San Marco note.

title: Mutual respect with Savonarola
summary: Crinito's account frames the relationship as affectionate and intellectually serious, not merely master-disciple.

title: Ficino and Pico diverge
summary: Scholarship notes a divergence between Ficino and Pico around this period, especially in relation to astrology and religious orientation.
source_note: Walden note citing Weinstein and Edelheit.

title: Death of Lorenzo de' Medici
summary: Lorenzo's death changes the political and patronage environment in Florence during Pico's final years.
source_note: General chronology; connect to Florentine context in close reading.

title: Medici protection weakens
summary: The death of Lorenzo alters the conditions under which Pico's post-trial life and Savonarola's influence unfold.

title: Compiles work against astrology with Savonarola's help
summary: Savonarola assists Pico in compiling his work against astrology.

title: Anti-astrology work links Pico and Savonarola
summary: The Disputationes adversus astrologiam divinatricem becomes a key text for assessing shared religious concern and differences from Ficino.

title: Pico considers Dominican life
summary: Pico dispenses with worldly wealth and considers joining the Dominican order, though he never takes vows.

title: Late ascetic turn intensifies
summary: Pico's late religious posture becomes central to hagiographic and revisionist readings of his life.
source_note: Copenhaver Magic and Dignity chapter 'Pico Sainted'; Walden.

title: Disputationes near completion/publication context
summary: Pico's anti-astrology work belongs to his final year and later reception as an attack on judicial astrology.

title: Pico's death fixes an unfinished career
summary: Pico's early death leaves his project fragmentary, making later scholarship especially dependent on editions, letters, and reception narratives.
source_note: Howlett preface notes incomplete career and puzzles.

title: French invasion reaches Florence
summary: Charles VIII's Italian campaign creates the political crisis surrounding Florence in Pico's last days.
source_note: General chronology; verify against Florence/Savonarola context.

title: Burial at San Marco
summary: After his death, Pico is buried at San Marco in the Dominican habit at his request and by Savonarola's hands.

title: Death in Florence
summary: Pico dies in Florence at age thirty-one.
source_note: Basic chronology; verify exact date in life chapter.

title: Savonarola's Compendium appears
summary: Savonarola's Compendium of Revelations becomes a comparison point for claims about shared theological imagery and reform concerns.

title: Posthumous editing begins shaping Pico
summary: Posthumous editorial work by family and Piagnoni circles becomes part of the problem of defining the authentic Pico.
source_note: Walden note on posthumous tampering and Farmer.

title: Savonarola executed
summary: Savonarola's execution changes the reception of Pico's late religious associations and the political-theological memory of Florence.
source_note: General chronology; connect through Savonarola scholarship.

title: Later anti-astrology print pairing
summary: A later Savonarola work on astrology is printed and cited as corroborated by Pico.

title: Cassirer frames Pico in Renaissance ideas
summary: Ernst Cassirer's article becomes one twentieth-century landmark in constructing Pico as a Renaissance philosophical figure.

title: English anthology canonizes the Oration package
summary: The widely used On the Dignity of Man / On Being and One / Heptaplus anthology helps shape Anglophone access to Pico.
source_note: Heptaplus anthology in corpus; verify publication metadata.

title: Wirszubski foregrounds Jewish mysticism
summary: Wirszubski and Kristeller's study becomes a major landmark for Pico's encounter with Jewish mysticism.

title: Farmer's 900 Theses project
summary: Stephen Farmer's Syncretism in the West provides a major modern edition/interpretive framework for the 900 Theses.

title: Allen collected studies
summary: Michael J. B. Allen's collected studies consolidate work on Ficino-Pico Platonism and the controversies between them.

title: Copenhaver's Magic and the Dignity of Man
summary: Copenhaver reframes the Oration through modern memory, dignity, magic, Kabbalah, and the history of Pico's afterlives.

title: Howlett's Re-evaluating Pico
summary: Howlett revisits Pico through Aristotelianism, Kabbalism, Platonism, and the changing field of Pico studies.

title: Copenhaver's Pico on Trial
summary: Copenhaver analyzes the heresy case, scholastic logic, freedom, and the thirteen conclusions.
